frameworks.name: retrospective Bayesian decoding
frameworks.one_line: higher-level ordinal judgment retrospectively decodes lower-level absolute orientation memory

frameworks.name: low-to-high hierarchical decoding
frameworks.one_line: default view that decoding mirrors low-to-high encoding hierarchy

frameworks.name: reversed-hierarchy / global-precedence theories
frameworks.one_line: descriptive accounts of high-to-low or bidirectional perceptual processing

frameworks.name: working memory in perception
frameworks.one_line: decoding requires noisy working memory across sequential inspections

core_question: Does perceptual decoding proceed low-to-high like encoding, or do higher-level features constrain lower-level ones retrospectively?
key_assumptions: encoding progresses from low- to high-level features
higher-level features are more invariant, categorical, and behaviorally relevant
working memory of absolute features is noisy
manipulations: absolute versus relative/ordinal judgments of orientation
two sequentially presented orientations within a trial
key_findings: absolute single-orientation judgments fail to account for relative/ordinal judgments
retrospective decoding by ordinal judgment explains correlation between reported orientations
retrospective decoding explains forward and backward aftereffects within a trial
interpretations: brain prioritizes higher-level decoding because it is more reliable and behaviorally relevant
reliable higher-level decoding retrospectively constrains less reliable lower-level decoding
